questions.question: What unavoidable tension do single-tokenizer unified models face?
options: GPUs cannot run two losses at once

Understanding wants semantic features (SigLIP/DINOv2 concept-level vectors) while generation wants reconstruction-friendly VQ codes that compose back into crisp pixels — one encoder cannot optimize for both
Image tokens overflow the context window
Licensing conflicts between encoder vendors
questions.explanation: Chameleon, Show-o, and Transfusion all share one visual tokenizer across both directions and pay a visible quality tax on one of them. VQ tokens have weak semantic coherence; SigLIP embeddings don't reconstruct.

questions.question: What is Janus-Pro's architectural fix?
options: A bigger single encoder
Decoupled encoders sharing one transformer body: understanding routes through SigLIP-SO400m + a 2-layer MLP, generation routes through a VQ tokenizer on input and a VQ decoder on output
Two complete separate models ensembled
Dropping the vision encoder entirely
questions.explanation: Everything upstream and downstream of the shared body is task-specific. Routing is disambiguated by prompt format (<understand> vs <generate> tags) or implicitly from the task.

questions.question: Why does decoupling improve BOTH directions at once?
options: It halves the parameter count

Understanding loss gets features tuned for semantic similarity (better perception than Show-o/Transfusion) while generation loss gets codes tuned for reconstruction (cleaner pixels) — the shared body learns to absorb the two input distributions
It eliminates the need for training data
Decoupling speeds up attention quadratically
questions.explanation: The claim: with enough data and parameters, the body handles the switching. Each task simply receives the representation its loss actually wants.

questions.question: What separated Janus-Pro from the original Janus?
options: A completely new architecture
Scale: 7B params (vs 1.3B), stage-1 alignment data 72M→90M pairs, stage-2 unified data 26M→72M, plus 200k image-gen instruction samples in stage 3 — the decoupling idea was already in Janus
Switching from SigLIP to CLIP
Removing the VQ tokenizer
questions.explanation: Result: Janus-Pro-7B matches LLaVA on MMMU (60.3 vs ~58) and beats DALL-E 3 on GenEval (0.80 vs 0.67) — one open model competitive on both sides of the unified spectrum.

questions.question: How do the three unified-model architecture families divide the design space?
options: They differ only in parameter count
Coupled-discrete (Show-o: one VQ tokenizer both ways), coupled-continuous (Transfusion: continuous patches + diffusion loss), and decoupled (Janus-Pro: SigLIP for understanding, VQ for generation)
All three use decoupled encoders
They are the same architecture from three labs
questions.explanation: The axis is what representation each direction sees. Janus-Pro's answer to the tokenizer compromise is to stop compromising — different needs, different encoders, shared brain.
